rationale.R1.1: The contribution claim—that the (reasoning-step, tool-call) pair is a novel scheduling unit with explicit trajectory lifecycle—must be precisely reconstructed to judge whether the positioning is coherent and not subtly circular or over-scoped.
rationale.R1.2: Each of the three contrast arguments rests on a stated premise about what prior systems assume (stateless requests, static DAGs, IDL-declared call graphs). Those premises must logically entail the claimed differentiation; any mismatch collapses a pillar of the positioning.
rationale.R1.3: The artifact makes factual characterizations of prior-system behavior (e.g., 'step durations dominated by I/O,' 'latency tail dominated by token generation') that function as implicit evidence; loose alignment between these characterizations and documented system behavior is peripherally relevant.
rationale.R1.4: This is the load-bearing dimension. The delegation is a novelty-assessment task in its entirety. The terminal claim—'no prior system has been built around this scheduling unit'—must survive scrutiny against nearest neighbors (e.g., vLLM, SGLang, Temporal, AWS Step Functions, Dapr) and any hybrid systems that partially overlap the claimed design-space point.
rationale.R1.7: The artifact describes three bodies of prior work without a single citation. Accurate attribution and adequate coverage of specific representative systems are required for the positioning to be scholarly credible; missing or mischaracterized references directly undermine the novelty argument.
